stories.title: Walker Webcast: Willy Walker Cuts Through the Market Noise
stories.excerpt: In the post-pandemic years of 2023 and 2024, many in commercial real estate believed that although the economy wasn’t great and there wasn’t much money to be made, “survive until ’25” was a mantra to live by.  “Unfortunately, we all survived to ’25 and then nothing had happened,” Walker & Dunlop chairman and CEO Willy Walker told the audience in his introductory remarks at the company’s Summer Conference.  Although the market is beginning to rebound a year later, the rate of progress isn’t aligning with anyone’s projections. “The saying now seems to be, ‘there will be a slice of heaven in ’27,’ ” Walker continued. In the current environment, said Walker, it’s important to cut through the noise. “What is it that the data is actually telling us?” He said. “It feels as if every one of us has an Instagram feed that is filled with noise. It’s clickbait, it’s fun to look at, but it doesn’t really tell you anything.”  Walker aimed to cut through some of that noise, noting that the employment rate has remained steady, belying fears of artificial intelligence taking away jobs. Nonetheless, public opposition to AI and data centers has persisted, with about $131 billion of data center projects turned down by local municipalities in the first quarter of 2026. “While AI is ready to go, the government is going to come in and it’s going to slow it down,” said Walker.  Another factor is how the U.S. is faring from a competitive standpoint against China when it comes to AI usage. The month-to-month growth the use of token is one metric: China more than doubled from 46 trillion tokens in May to 98 trillion in June,
bullets: Walker & Dunlop CEO: Recovery slower than expected; "slice of heaven in '27" now mantra.
Employment steady despite AI fears; $131B data center projects rejected by municipalities Q1 2026.
Government regulatory delays will slow AI/data center deployment despite tech readiness.
stories.summary: Willy Walker, chairman of Walker & Dunlop, warned that despite predictions of a 2025 rebound, CRE market recovery is lagging projections and sentiment has shifted to expecting improvement in 2027. Employment remains resilient, contradicting AI job-displacement fears, but regulatory opposition is a major brake: $131 billion of data center projects were rejected by local municipalities in Q1 2026. Walker emphasized cutting through noise to focus on actual data rather than sentiment.
stories.impact: Regulatory headwinds on data center approvals directly constrain supply-driven demand in tech hubs. Multifamily developers should monitor municipal pushback on large infrastructure projects, as anti-data-center sentiment may bleed into zoning and entitlement friction for residential development in those same markets. Delayed commercial/industrial growth also dampens migration-driven apartment demand.

stories.title: AI Firm Mercor Brings One World Trade to 97% Leased
stories.excerpt: The Durst Organization and The Port Authority of New York and New Jersey announced that Mercor has signed a lease at One World Trade Center for 25,550 square feet on the 77th floor. The term length was not disclosed. With this agreement, One World Trade Center is 97% leased and Mercor joins the growing community of artificial intelligence companies with offices at the 3.1-million-square-foot tower. “One World Trade Center was built for forward-looking, cutting-edge companies looking for a world-class home in New York City. We look forward to welcoming Mercor to the building,” said Jody Durst, president of The Durst Organization. The Durst Organization was represented in-house by Eric Engelhardt, Karen Rose, and Sayo Kamara and by Newmark’s David Falk, Peter Shimkin, Hal Stein, Nathan Kropp, and Paige Raisides. Harry Singer of CBRE represented Mercor. The post AI Firm Mercor Brings One World Trade to 97% Leased appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mercor (AI firm) leases 25,550 sq ft at One World Trade Center, 77th floor.
One World Trade Center now 97% leased; AI tenants clustering in the 3.1M sf tower.
Growing AI office footprint signals strong demand for premium, trophy office in Manhattan CBD.
stories.summary: AI firm Mercor signed a 25,550-square-foot lease at One World Trade Center on the 77th floor, bringing the 3.1-million-square-foot tower to 97% occupancy. The deal reflects a broader pattern of AI companies establishing Manhattan offices, with the Durst Organization noting the tower has become a hub for forward-looking tech firms.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, clustering of AI employment in lower Manhattan CBD may support elevated rents for downtown residential nearby (NoLita, TriBeCa, Financial District), but office strength does not reliably translate to apartment demand without sustained headcount growth and wage inflation.

stories.title: Historic Cinerama Dome, Adjacent 14-Screen Complex Set for 2028 Reopening
stories.excerpt: Shuttered since the pandemic, the historic Cinerama Dome in Hollywood and surrounding 14-screen movie theater are set to reopen under the management of Alamo Drafthouse Cinema, a unit of Sony Pictures Entertainment. CBRE’s Nicole Mihalka, along with Carl Muhlstein of Muhlstein CRE, and Scott Burns and Shauna Mattis of JLL jointly represented the ownership of the Dome Entertainment Center, while JLL’s Ken Shishido represented Alamo Drafthouse. Opened in 1963, the Dome will undergo restorative renovations starting next month through early 2028. Its historic legacy and singular moviegoing experience will be honored and carefully preserved, with the iconic “Cinerama” name and exterior branding left intact. The adjacent complex, formerly run by ArcLight Cinemas, will be operated as an Alamo Drafthouse dine-in cinema. “This lease marks the beginning of an exciting new chapter for one of Hollywood’s most iconic and beloved properties,” said Mihalka. “The Cinerama Dome has been a cultural landmark for generations, and bringing Sony Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse to the Dome Entertainment Center is a strong vote of confidence in the future of theatrical entertainment and the continued demand for shared moviegoing experiences.” The post Historic Cinerama Dome, Adjacent 14-Screen Complex Set for 2028 Reopening appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cinerama Dome and 14-screen ArcLight complex set to reopen early 2028 under Alamo Drafthouse.
Sony Pictures Entertainment backing long-term lease; restorations begin next month through early 2028.
Historic Hollywood venue reopening signals confidence in experiential entertainment and traffic return to Sunset Boulevard.
stories.summary: Sony Pictures Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse Cinema announced a long-term lease to restore and reopen the shuttered Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen ArcLight Cinemas complex in Hollywood, both dark since the pandemic. Renovations begin next month with reopening targeted for early 2028, preserving the 86-foot curved screen and iconic branding.
stories.impact: Tangential to multifamily. Entertainment venue reopening on Sunset Boulevard signals foot traffic and vibrancy recovery to Hollywood corridor, which could support nearby residential leasing and values, but the primary driver remains broader employment and wage growth in Los Angeles, not theater attendance.

stories.title: Roselle Park Apartments Trade After Brokers Resolve Permit Issue
stories.excerpt: Roselle Grande, a 119-unit garden-style apartment community located at 220-250 W. Sumner Ave. in Roselle Park, NJ, has changed hands for an undisclosed price, in a transaction arranged by SAGE Real Estate Advisors. The firm’s brokerage team of partners and co-founders Steve Tragash and Greg Pine, alongside principal Eli Herskowitz and associate Katie Migner, exclusively represented the seller, Roselle Grande LLC, and procured the unnamed buyer. During the seller’s deliverables phase, the transaction encountered an unresolved permit issue. Recognizing the importance of a coordinated effort, SAGE spearheaded a resolution for which Tragash and Migner spent nearly a month consulting with and engaging engineering companies to provide the town’s construction department with required documentation. “Today’s commercial real estate environment requires an engaged and knowledgeable advisor who is willing to step outside the traditional box,” said Tragash. Built in 1970 and situated on a parcel at the intersection of Roselle Park and Kenilworth, Roselle Grande consists of four buildings with a mix of studio, one-bedroom and two-bedroom layouts. The stabilized property features historically high occupancy rates, on-site parking and dedicated laundry facilities. The post Roselle Park Apartments Trade After Brokers Resolve Permit Issue appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Roselle Grande, 119-unit NJ garden complex, trades hands; broker resolved permit issue during sale.
Built 1970, four buildings, mixed unit types; stabilized with high occupancy and on-site amenities.
Permit dispute resolution required month of broker-led engineering coordination with town construction department.
stories.summary: Roselle Grande, a 119-unit garden-style apartment community in Roselle Park, New Jersey, changed hands in an undisclosed transaction brokered by SAGE Real Estate Advisors. The 1970-built, four-building property features studio, one- and two-bedroom units with historically high occupancy. The sale encountered a permit issue that required broker-led coordination with municipal engineering consultants to resolve.
stories.impact: Illustrates ongoing regulatory friction in NJ municipal approval processes that can delay or complicate closings on stabilized assets. Developers pursuing acquisitions in northern New Jersey suburbs should budget extra timeline and broker expertise for permit/documentation resolution, particularly on older properties.

stories.title: Jon Pezzoni Joins Hunneman’s Leasing & Advisory Team as SVP
stories.excerpt: Hunneman said Wednesday that Jon Pezzoni has joined the firm’s Leasing & Advisory team as an SVP and Principal. He brings more than a decade of experience across Greater Boston’s office, R&D and industrial markets. Pezzoni was most recently an SVP at Avison Young. Since 2014, Pezzoni has been advising tenants, landlords, and developers throughout the Metro West, 495 West & South submarkets, devising and executing strategies that best position assets to maximize their value. “Jon has built an outstanding reputation throughout the Metro West market by consistently delivering exceptional results for his clients,” said Steve Prozinski, CEO & COO of Hunneman. “His expertise across office, R&D, and industrial properties, combined with his collaborative approach and deep local relationships, make him an excellent addition to our Leasing & Advisory team. We’re excited to welcome Jon to Hunneman and look forward to the value he’ll bring to both our clients and our firm.” The post Jon Pezzoni Joins Hunneman’s Leasing & Advisory Team as SVP appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Jon Pezzoni joins Hunneman as SVP, bringing 10+ years Greater Boston office/R&D/industrial experience.
Pezzoni previously SVP at Avison Young; deep expertise in Metro West, 495 West, South submarkets.
Hire signals Hunneman expansion in Boston-area office and industrial brokerage amid market consolidation.
stories.summary: Hunneman announced Jon Pezzoni joined as SVP and Principal on its Leasing & Advisory team. Pezzoni brings over a decade of experience across Greater Boston's office, R&D, and industrial markets, with a track record advising tenants, landlords, and developers in Metro West, 495 West, and South submarkets. He was previously an SVP at Avison Young.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. Reflects talent consolidation in Boston-area CRE brokerage, which may signal market maturation or competitive pressure in office/industrial. Not relevant to multifamily developers unless they are seeking tenant advisory or financing arranged in the Boston region.

stories.title: Meal Delivery Service CookUnity to Open Flagship Storefront at NoMad Hotel
stories.excerpt: CookUnity, a subscription-based meal delivery platform, has inked 4,721 square feet of ground-floor retail space at Lam Group’s 1227 Broadway, also known as Virgin Hotel New York City, in Manhattan’s NoMad neighborhood, Commercial Observer has learned. The deal represents the chef-prepared meal service company’s first flagship storefront in New York City, a source close to the deal told CO. The store is expected to open in the first half of 2027. CookUnity launched in 2015 and is headquartered in Brooklyn at 630 Flushing Avenue. The company also has an existing Manhattan kitchen hub at 534 West 50th Street in Hell’s Kitchen and a Flatiron District office at 36 Lexington Avenue. It’s unclear whether its new NoMad spot represents a fourth location or a relocation of an existing site. The asking rent and the length of the lease were also not available. The average asking rent for retail space in the nearby neighborhood around Herald Square was $394 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. The landlord was represented by Adam Weinblatt and Harley Bonn from Newmark, according to the source. CookUnity was represented by Alexandra Yanoff and Christine Nebiar from Brand Urban. Newmark declined to comment. “We’re excited to see CookUnity’s first flagship land at the Virgin Hotel in NoMad, a great signal for the brand’s evolution from delivery only to a space where people can finally interact with it in real life through fine dining and retail,” Nebiar said in a statement. CookUnity’s new flagship will feature dining and retail options, as well as space for community gatherings for lovers of the culinary arts. The company delivers in New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, Miami, Atlanta, Austin, Chicago, Seattle and Toronto. Virgin, a 38-story hotel tower on Broadway between West 29th and West 30th streets, opened in 2023 and provides guests with over 400 hotel rooms, a rooftop pool and restaurant Everdene, which o
bullets: CookUnity meal-delivery signs 4,721 sq ft ground-floor retail at Virgin Hotel NoMad (1227 Broadway).
Flagship NYC storefront opening H1 2027; average NoMad retail asking rent ~$394/sf (Q2 2026).
Mixed-use/retail activation at hotel continues NoMad's role as premium food-service and hospitality cluster.
stories.summary: CookUnity, a subscription meal-delivery platform, leased 4,721 square feet of ground-floor retail at 1227 Broadway (Virgin Hotel New York) in Manhattan's NoMad neighborhood, marking the company's first flagship NYC storefront, expected to open in H1 2027. The deal underscores NoMad's evolution into a premium food and hospitality destination. Average asking rent in the Herald Square area was $394 per square foot in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. Retail activation at high-end hotels supports neighborhood foot traffic and vibrancy, which can modestly support adjacent residential appeal and rents, but is secondary to underlying demand drivers like employment, income, and household formation.

stories.title: It’s Finally Showtime Again at Hollywood’s Cinerama Dome
stories.excerpt: Get your popcorn ready, Los Angeles. One of Hollywood’s most beloved movie theaters is finally coming back. Sony Pictures Entertainment and its Alamo Drafthouse Cinema subsidiary announced Wednesday they struck a long-term lease to restore and reopen the Cinerama Dome on Sunset Boulevard and the adjacent 14-screen former ArcLight Cinemas complex, six years after both went dark during the pandemic. “Hot damn! We’re bringing the Dome back, baby,” Tom Rothman, chairman and CEO of Sony’s Motion Picture Group, said in a statement. “We believe in moviegoing down to our soul, and no venue on Earth stands more for that than the one-of-a-kind Dome. I am deeply proud that Sony is investing in Hollywood, literally.” The Cinerama Dome is expected to reopen in early 2028, with renovations beginning next month to preserve its 86-foot-wide curved screen and large-format presentation. JLL’s Ken Shishido represented Alamo Drafthouse in the lease. CBRE’s Nicole Mihalka, Muhlstein Commercial’s Carl Muhlstein and JLL’s Scott Burns and Shauna Mattis represented the ownership of the Dome Entertainment Center. Alamo will operate the landmark, but the Dome will not offer dine-in service like at typical Alamo theaters. The neighboring former ArcLight multiplex will reopen as an Alamo Drafthouse with dine-in service, 4K laser projection and 70-millimeter cinematic capabilities. Sony acquired Alamo Drafthouse in 2024. The deal carries far more significance in addition to filling a long-vacant theater complex. “This lease marks the beginning of an exciting new chapter for one of Hollywood’s most iconic and beloved properties,” Mihalka said in a statement. “The Cinerama Dome has been a cultural landmark for generations, and bringing Sony Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse to the Dome Entertainment Center is a strong vote of confidence in the future of theatrical entertainment and the continued demand for shared moviegoing experiences.” The unmistakable landmark opened in 1963. It’s a Los
bullets: Sony Pictures Entertainment, Alamo Drafthouse reopen Cinerama Dome and 14-screen complex early 2028.
Long-term lease reflects studio confidence in theatrical exhibition and shared experiential entertainment demand.
Sunset Boulevard venue reopening signals Hollywood neighborhood revitalization post-pandemic.
stories.summary: Sony Pictures Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse Cinema announced plans to restore and reopen the iconic Cinerama Dome on Sunset Boulevard and the adjacent 14-screen ArcLight Cinemas, both shuttered during the pandemic. Sony is making a direct investment to restore the venue's historic 86-foot curved screen. Reopening is targeted for early 2028.
stories.impact: No material multifamily impact. Venue reopening supports neighborhood traffic and cultural appeal in Hollywood corridor, but does not generate direct apartment demand or rents without concurrent employment or population growth in the submarket.

stories.title: Bascom Buys 183-Unit Orange County, Calif., Complex for $53M
stories.excerpt: The Bascom Group has acquired a 183-unit apartment complex in Orange County, Calif., as the company expands its multifamily holdings this year in the Western U.S. The Irvine, Calif.-based investment firm put down $53.1 million for Castlewood Park Apartments in Buena Park. The deal is equal to about $290,200 per unit. Institutional Property Advisors (IPA), a division of Marcus & Millichap, announced the deal after the brokerage’s Brian Eisendrath, Cameron Chalfant, Jesse Zarouk and Jake Vitta arranged the financing. IPA’s Kevin Green, Joe Grabiec and Gregory Harris represented the seller, which was not disclosed. Property records show Newcastle owned the property for more than 30 years. IPA described Buena Park as one of the most supply-constrained submarkets in Orange County. “Castlewood Park is the largest multifamily sale to transact in Buena Park and one of only eight $50 million-plus multifamily sales in north Orange County in the last 24 months,” Green said in a statement. Built in 1963, Castlewood Park includes 46 buildings across 8.7 acres at 6968 Campus Drive, adjacent to Buena Park High School. The community features of two-, three- and four-bedroom apartments averaging 1,028 square feet. It was renovated a few times from 2010 through 2023. Bascom plans to renovate apartment interiors, upgrade the property’s amenities, and add full-time on-site management. Bascom said it has acquired 13 apartment communities totaling 3,231 units for more than $930 million since federal interest rates started rising after the pandemic. Earlier this year, Bascom acquired a 294-unit property in Las Vegas and a 289-unit in Tucson, Ariz. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bascom Group acquires Castlewood Park, 183-unit Orange County complex, for $53.1M ($290k per unit).
1963-built property in Buena Park, one of North Orange County's 8 largest $50M+ deals in 24 months.
Buena Park characterized as supply-constrained submarket; largest multifamily sale ever in that city.
stories.summary: The Bascom Group acquired Castlewood Park Apartments, a 183-unit garden-style complex in Buena Park, Orange County, for $53.1 million ($290,200 per unit). The 1970-built property spans 8.7 acres across 46 buildings with studio, one-, and two-bedroom layouts. IPA noted Buena Park is one of Orange County's most supply-constrained submarkets and this was the largest multifamily transaction in the city's history.
stories.impact: Supply constraints in Buena Park North Orange County submarket are supporting pricing for stabilized assets and likely creating unmet demand for new rental units. Developers with Orange County land should prioritize Buena Park and north county submarkets where supply is tight and investor appetite is demonstrably strong; limited new construction pipeline suggests room for new class-A product.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital Group files data center permit for Plaza 500, Alexandria VA; 226k+ sq ft Phase 1.
Proposed substation within 100 ft of homes sparks resident opposition; neighborhood rally planned for September.
Fairfax County rejected Starwood's alternative Chantilly site proposal; development proceeding despite local pushback.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for a two-building data center project at Plaza 500 on the Alexandria-Fairfax County line in Northern Virginia, with Phase 1 comprising 226,000+ square feet. The project includes a proposed electrical substation within 100 feet of nearby homes, triggering resident opposition and a planned September rally against the planning commission. Fairfax County rejected Starwood's proposal to relocate the project to an alternate Chantilly site.
stories.impact: Regulatory and community opposition to data center infrastructure will likely slow permitting and construction timelines in Northern Virginia, constraining the region's ability to attract and retain AI/tech employers. Multifamily developers should expect slower job growth in Fairfax/Alexandria submarkets if data center development faces extended delays; reduced employment pipeline will dampen future apartment demand and rent growth forecasts for the region.

stories.title: JP Morgan Recaps Industrial Portfolio With $209M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group has landed a $208.5 million debt package to recapitalize 40 industrial assets in Pennsylvania and South Florida, Commercial Observer has learned. J.P. Morgan Chase supplied the five-year, floating-rate loan on the sponsorship’s logistics portfolio comprising 2.3 million square feet and featuring a mix of last-mile warehouses, distribution sites and shallow-bay facilities. The properties, which have buildings ranging from 16,000 to 155,000 square feet, are 95 percent leased to 110 tenants. CBRE negotiated the debt with a team consisting of Tom Traynor, Tom Rugg, Mark Finan and Henry Fenmore. J.P. Morgan, Centerbridge and Henderson Group did not immediately return requests for comment. CBRE declined to comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Centerbridge/Henderson JV secures $208.5M debt to recapitalize 40 industrial assets in PA and South Florida.
Portfolio: 2.3M sq ft, 95% leased to 110 tenants; 5-year floating-rate loan from J.P. Morgan Chase.
Mix of last-mile, distribution, shallow-bay facilities; buildings range 16k–155k sq ft.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group landed a $208.5 million debt package from J.P. Morgan Chase to recapitalize 40 industrial assets totaling 2.3 million square feet across Pennsylvania and South Florida. The portfolio is 95% leased to 110 tenants and comprises last-mile warehouses, distribution centers, and shallow-bay facilities ranging from 16,000 to 155,000 square feet.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. Industrial debt activity and strong occupancy (95%) signal healthy logistics/e-commerce demand in South Florida and Pennsylvania, which supports employment and population in those regions. Multifamily developers should monitor South Florida industrial strength as a proxy for job growth and in-migration that will support apartment demand in Miami-Dade and Broward County.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: OKO Group and Shinsegae Group form $500M fund for luxury hotels, residences, clubs across Asia/Middle East/US.
Initial project: mixed-use Aman development in Seoul's Gangnam District; Aman sold $9B branded residences in 4 years.
Aman Beverly Hills penthouses targeting $200M sale; project already secured $1B+ in condo reservations.
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korean retail conglomerate Shinsegae Group formed a $500 million fund to develop luxury hotels, residences, and clubs across Asia, the Middle East, and the United States. The first project is a mixed-use Aman property in Seoul's Gangnam District. Doronin noted Aman has sold $9 billion in branded residences over four years, and the under-construction Aman Beverly Hills has secured $1 billion in condo reservations.
stories.impact: Ultra-luxury residential pipeline (Aman Beverly Hills, Seoul) targets high-net-worth buyers and does not compete with mainstream multifamily. However, the capital infusion and international expansion signal growing investor confidence in branded luxury residential globally, which may support valuations and financing availability for class-A and luxury apartment developments in gateway markets.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B nontraded apartment REIT; 45 properties, 5,600+ units; includes 8 Carlisle.
8 Carlisle: 462-unit Financial District tower; $377M construction loan from Maxim Capital + Skylight mezzanine.
Grubb secured $617M total financing (construction + $240M credit facility) to enable larger balance sheet and bigger debt.
stories.summary: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT by consolidating multiple funds into a nontraded REIT with 45 properties and 5,600+ units valued at approximately $1.9 billion. The portfolio includes 8 Carlisle, a 462-unit rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District, which secured a $377 million construction loan (including $300 million from Maxim Capital and $77 million mezzanine from Skylight). Grubb also obtained a $240 million credit facility, bringing total financing to $617 million.
stories.impact: Large-cap multifamily sponsor consolidation into REIT structure signals confidence in apartment valuations and appetite for larger development portfolios. 8 Carlisle's strong financing (especially mezzanine availability) indicates lender willingness to support institutional-quality Manhattan construction. Developers with scale and pipeline should monitor REIT formations as a path to unlock balance-sheet capacity for growth; the model may become more prevalent as sponsors seek larger equity sources and more efficient capital stacks.

stories.title: CDK Lending Supplies $37M Loan for Newark Apartments Project
stories.excerpt: RBH Group has landed $36.7 million of pre-development financing for a mixed-use multifamily project in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. CDK Lending provided the loan on the developer’s planned 420-unit apartment complex with ground-floor retail space at 101 Market Street in Newark’s Four Corners neighborhood near downtown. The debt pays off a previous loan provided by Goldman Sachs for the long-planned development, which is located within a 5.65-acre land assemblage site. Meridian Capital Group arranged the transaction with a team led by David Bollag and Naphtali Marrus. “Newark’s downtown has seen remarkable momentum over the past decade, and a development-ready assemblage of this scale in the central business district is exceptionally hard to come by,” Bollag said in a statement. RBH Group has been assembling land for a mixed-use development consisting of 49 separate contiguous lots since 2005, according to Bollag. The firm also plans to commence a 241-unit multifamily project at nearby 193 Market Street next year and is eyeing other developments at the site roughly half a mile from Newark Penn Station. Newark-based RBH Group is also developing the Teachers Village project at 243 Halsey Street in Newark featuring 203 apartments geared toward local educators. CDK Lending and RBH Group did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: RBH Group secures $36.7M pre-development financing for 420-unit Newark mixed-use project, 101 Market Street.
CDK Lending provides loan; Goldman Sachs previous lender; site spans 5.65 acres, 49 contiguous lots since 2005.
Four Corners neighborhood near downtown Newark; ground-floor retail planned; site assemblage exceptional rarity in CBD.
stories.summary: RBH Group landed $36.7 million in pre-development financing from CDK Lending for a 420-unit mixed-use apartment complex with ground-floor retail at 101 Market Street in Newark's Four Corners neighborhood. The loan replaces a previous Goldman Sachs facility. The 5.65-acre assemblage comprises 49 contiguous lots accumulated since 2005—described as exceptionally rare in Newark's central business district.
stories.impact: Newark downtown showing momentum and lender appetite for large-scale residential development. The assemblage achievement and pre-dev financing signal market confidence in Newark's revitalization. Developers with significant land in Newark CBD should accelerate entitlements and financing; the market is primed for institutional-quality multifamily with rare land bases. Job growth and transit proximity (near downtown) support long-term lease-up and rent growth potential.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC permit filings week of July 17, 2026: 112-unit mixed-use (Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts), 86-unit mixed-use (Queens Vernon Blvd).
Bronx: two 9-story, 99-unit buildings each at Tremont Avenue/Prospect Avenue (198 units combined).
Financial District major alteration (Brookfield Properties, 250 Vestry Street, 192k sq ft).
stories.summary: NYC construction permits filed week of July 17, 2026 included a 112-unit mixed-use project at 103 Empire Boulevard (Crown Heights–Prospect-Lefferts border), an 86-unit, 10-story mixed-use on Vernon Boulevard in Queens, and two new 99-unit apartment buildings in the Bronx's Tremont section. Major alteration work included 192,245 square feet at 250 Vestry Street (Financial District) by Brookfield Properties.
stories.impact: Steady multifamily filing activity across outer boroughs (Bronx, Queens) and mixed-use integration in central Brooklyn indicates sustained development appetite despite financing headwinds. Permit pace suggests Q1-Q2 2027 starts likely; monitor supply pipeline for outer-borough absorption and rent trajectory. Financial District alteration signals continued office-to-residential conversion interest in lower Manhattan.

stories.title: Related Urban targets aging Coconut Grove housing for two-tower project
stories.excerpt: Related Urban Development Group wants to replace the majority of an aging public housing complex in Coconut Grove with high-rise apartments that contain some affordable housing under Florida’s Live Local Act. The Miami-based affordable housing subsidiary of Related Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower multifamily project with some retail on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County-owned site at 3150 and 3160 Mundy Street and 3181 Douglas Road, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The proposal seeks to demolish a 100-unit apartment building completed in 1975 and a 65-unit building completed in 1982, while preserving a 68-unit apartment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Urban Development plans Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza: 750 units, two towers, Coconut Grove Miami.
Project demolishes two aging public housing buildings (100 units, 1975; 65 units, 1982); preserves one 68-unit building.
Uses Florida Live Local Act (40%+ affordable required); near Douglas Road Metrorail; Miami-Dade-owned site, county review.
stories.summary: Related Urban Development Group filed plans for Magnus Grove and Stirrup Plaza, a two-tower, 750-unit mixed-income project on a 6.1-acre Miami-Dade County site in Coconut Grove. The development will demolish 165 units of aging public housing (from 1975 and 1982) while preserving a 68-unit building. The 22- and 25-story towers will include 750 apartments, retail, a library, and parking, utilizing Florida's Live Local Act to bypass local zoning restrictions; at least 40% of units must be below-market-rate.
stories.impact: Large-scale public housing replacement using state affordability incentive signals Miami-Dade's commitment to mixed-income residential and density near transit (Douglas Road Metrorail). Related Urban's strategy demonstrates how Live Local Act compliance can unlock value in aging public assets. Developers with similar public/quasi-public land positions in Miami-Dade should expedite Live Local applications; county approval pathway is faster and Live Local density bonuses offset affordable-unit cost burden. Coconut Grove revitalization adds to Miami's broader pipeline and may pressure rents in adjacent unsubsidized properties.

stories.title: BlackRock, MGX buy Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in $40B deal
stories.excerpt: A joint venture that includes BlackRock’s Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX is acquiring Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in a $40 billion deal. The group is also planning to invest $5 billion in the firm it bought from Macquarie Asset Management. The capital injection is primarily to help Aligned expand capacity, according to Bloomberg. Aligned, who owns and operates data centers across the Americas, including in Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil and Mexico, has more than 6.4 GW in operational and planned output capacity, according to the outlet. Acquiring Aligned was one of BlackRock’s largest infrastructure investments, and was called […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: BlackRock Global Infrastructure Partners and MGX (Abu Dhabi) acquire Aligned Data Centers for $40B.
Additional $5B investment injection for expansion; Aligned operates 6.4 GW capacity, 50 campuses, 78 data centers.
Texas power grid (ERCOT) constraints emerging concern as AI/data center demand drives rapid growth and local opposition.
stories.summary: A joint venture of BlackRock's Global Infrastructure Partners and Abu Dhabi-based MGX acquired Plano-based Aligned Data Centers in a $40 billion deal, with an additional $5 billion investment for expansion. Aligned operates more than 6.4 GW of operational and planned capacity across 50 campuses and 78 data centers in the Americas (Dallas, Atlanta, Chile, Brazil, Mexico). Rapid data center growth is raising concerns about power grid strain and local utility capacity, particularly in Texas under ERCOT oversight.
stories.impact: Massive data center capital influx into North Texas (Dallas/Plano) will drive AI/tech employment concentration and downstream apartment demand. However, ERCOT power-grid constraints and rising local opposition to data centers (as noted in Article 1) may throttle growth and limit sustained job creation. Multifamily developers should model Dallas demand upside but hedge for potential job-growth slowdown if power infrastructure and municipal permitting become bottlenecks; strong near-term tailwinds, but execution risk on longer-term pipeline.
